url: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=edJ6sSA6k4U
title: How Should Investors Trade the Bottom of the U-Shaped Economy?

Welcome back. Markets this morning are
lower on this final trading day of
September and the third quarter. Take a
look at futures indicating a decline of
about 130 on the Dow this morning. The
S&P down 13 and the Nasdaq down 45. All
three major indices finishing higher
yesterday, shrugging off any uncertainty
about a potential government shutdown.
Markets of course have been on a tear
this year. The Nasdaq is up 17% year-to-
date. The S&P 500 is up better than 13%
and the Dow is up about 9% for 2025,
largely fueled by the AI technology
boom. Meanwhile, the White House
releasing new details on tariffs going
into effect October 14th. That's 10%
tariff on imported lumber, a 25% tariff
on kitchen cabinets, vanities, and
upholstered furniture. On January 1st,
cabinets and vanities tariffs double to
as high as 50%. While upholstered wood
products will jump to 30%. And going
into effect tomorrow, 100% tariffs on
pharmaceutical companies who are not
manufacturing and doing supply chains in
America. Joining me now is 42 Macro
founder and CEO Darius Dale. Darius,
good to see you. Thank you so much for
being here. What is your market outlook
going into the fourth quarter?
>> Yeah. Yeah. So, we're incredibly bullish
heading into Q4 and beyond actually
because as we've talked about on your
show several times throughout this year,
the administration has chosen to outgrow
the debt with a com a mix of growth
friendly policies and in our opinion we
think markets are likely to be much
higher on a 12-month forward basis.
>> So, where do you want to be positioned
going into the fourth quarter? Do you
want to stick with what's been working?
And of course, that is technology.
>> Yeah, that's a great question. And I
think fourth quarter is going to
actually be quite challenging for
institutional investors because on one
hand we still continue to see the
U-shaped economy and slowing to the nar
of that U-shaped economy here in Q4 or
in Q4. But the reality is as we look
into 2026, it's highly likely that we
have a very robust cyclical recovery.
This is the other side of the U-shaped
economy you and I have been talking
about all year. And so at some point the
market the market internals will start
to rotate to more cyclical will start to
broaden out. And so that's going to be a
real challenge for institutional
investors to have to uh to have to
rotate into at some point over the next
three to four months.
>> Well, we're also looking at a macro
story that seems to be improving,
although questions around jobs. Take a
look at interest rates this morning.
We've got jobs data out all week uh
today as well as for the rest of the
week. Uh let's look at that labor data
out this week with the August jolt
report out today at 10 a.m. Eastern
ahead of the September ADP numbers out
tomorrow. expectations called for 50,000
private sector jobs added to the
economy. And then of course there's
Friday September jobs report from the
Labor Department where 50,000 jobs
created are expected. The unemployment
rate holding steady at 4.3%. But I've
got to add that the Labor Department
warned on Friday uh that the jobs report
may not even happen should the
government shut down at midnight. Are
you worried about a government shutdown?
>> Uh well actually no. No, I mean, our
research shows that there are only six
cycles that actually matter to impacting
the momentum and dispersion of asset
markets on a medium to long-term time
horizon. That's growth, that's
inflation, that's monetary policy,
that's fiscal policy, that's liquidity
and positioning. And it's highly
unlikely that a government shutdown,
however long it may last, materially
impacts those cycles and alters their
trajectory. So, in our opinion, we will
be buying any dips associated with with
any government shutdown.
>> Well, well, I guess it depends how long
it lasts, though, right?
>> Well, I'll say this. the longest
government shutdown in history, which
was uh December 2018 and January 2019.
We saw the S&P 500 rally 10% during the
government shutdown. We also saw it
rally another 10% in the three months
after the government shutdown. So
perhaps the government has shut down
more frequently.
>> So I again it was it was President
Trump's turn where the Democrats were
not going along with his ideas. Uh the
Fed uh cut rates once, but three Fed
presidents have offered different
signals on the central bank's path uh in
terms of rate cuts. Cleveland Fed
President Beth Hammock says that policy
is staying restrictive as inflation is
above the Fed's target. St. Louis Fed
President Alberto Misalum is keeping the
door open to further rate cuts but
remaining cautious. And New York Fed
President John Williams is pointing to
easing inflation risks and a softening
job market, saying the balance is
shifting. A cut in September made sense.
Do you think we'll see more cuts this
year?
>> Uh, in our opinion, it's likely that we
see more cuts because again, we're still
slowing to the narr of the U-shaped
economy. The labor market is certainly
taking a real significant step back
here. If you think of the last three
months of private sector labor income
growth, which is the sum product of
jobs, hours worked, and wages. We are
now the worst three months we've seen
since going back to late 2022, early
2023 when we had the technical
recession, regional banking crisis. So
it's our view that the labor market will
continue to get elevated in their
reaction function uh al maybe not to as
aggressive degree as uh Dr. Moran is
articulating but the one thing I will
continue to coach investors to focus on
over a long-term time horizon is that
there is structural regime change coming
for this institution. The next Fed chair
is going to be someone who tries to
legislate much lower term structure of
interest rates and you have to position
for that as an investor.
>> Well that's a good point and that's
obviously because President Trump will
be appointing that chairman. We spoke
with Stephen Meyer, the new governor on
the Federal Reserve appointed by
President Trump. He was here last week.
Here's what he said. Watch.
>> First of all, I think a lot of folks are
more concerned about tariffs driving
inflation than I am. I have not yet seen
evidence that there are uh tariff driven
increases in inflation. And it's really
important for monetary policy to exit
the quite restrictive stance that it's
in because the longer it stays in that
stance, the greater the risk to the
economy that you do get a material
downturn in the labor market that you
don't need to experience. In the coming
6 months, I'd like to see us get a lot
closer to neutral than we are now. My
view is that we can get there in in in a
very short series of 50 basis point
cuts, readjust monetary policy,
>> and do you feel that there's a a feeling
against a 50 basis points on the Fed
board?
Yeah. I mean, very clearly from the from
the outcome of last week's meeting,
people don't feel the need to move
urgently. Um, and part of that is
because they're still they're still very
scared of tariff inflation.
>> Yeah. So, they don't want to go 50.
They've been doing these uh 25 basis
point moves up or down, Darius.
>> Up or down in terms of the direction of
the interest rate?
>> Yeah. Well, the last obviously was
lower, but I'm saying that it's 25 basis
points versus 50.
>> Oh. Oh. Well, it's highly unlikely we do
50. There's going to be hardly an
economic justification for 50. But in
our opinion, that's exactly what you
don't want. You don't want to spook the
frogs that are being boiled alive in the
pot. That is the Treasury bond market
with regards to inflation. You want to
get the term structure of interest rate
down in a way that does not, you know,
sort of trigger, you know, significant
ampers um thought processes in the
market regarding inflation because
that's what will derail uh this bull
market.
>> All right, we will leave it there.
Darius, great to catch up. Thanks so
much Darius Dale joining us on investing
